description: Tuning a high-throughput job queue table that suffers from MVCC bloat. Review the indexing, fillfactor, autovacuum, and HOT-update implications.
review_checklist: Identify HOT update prerequisites (no indexed-column changes, fillfactor headroom)
Evaluate autovacuum tuning for write-heavy small tables
Verify partial index prerequisites for HOT-updates remain
Check bloat-reduction options: pg_repack, fillfactor, vacuum frequency

ground_truth.description: Adding a partial index on (queue_name, created_at) WHERE state = 'pending' helps dequeue but DEFEATS HOT updates for any UPDATE that changes state, queue_name, or created_at. HOT (Heap-Only Tuple) updates require: (a) sufficient fillfactor headroom, (b) no indexed columns changed. The state column change is the most frequent update on this table — every UPDATE state=... now does a non-HOT update, creating an index entry, accelerating bloat.

fillfactor not set on jobs table — defaults to 100% (every page packed full). For a table with 5-10 UPDATEs per row, no headroom means EVERY UPDATE creates a new tuple on a different page, breaking HOT and amplifying bloat. Set `ALTER TABLE jobs SET (fillfactor = 70)` to leave 30% per-page headroom for in-page UPDATE replacements. Should be bundled with this migration.

ALTER COLUMN payload SET STORAGE EXTENDED on a populated table queues a full table rewrite to re-toast every row's payload. On a 80GB bloated table this can take hours under AccessExclusiveLock. Even worse: STORAGE EXTENDED is the DEFAULT for JSONB — this statement is a no-op disguised as a config change, but still triggers the rewrite.

autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.2 means vacuum runs only after 20% of rows are dead — completely WRONG for this workload. With 50K active rows and 6000 jobs/sec, dead tuples accumulate at thousands/sec. Should be 0.01 or lower (vacuum after 1% bloat). Combined with autovacuum_vacuum_threshold for absolute trigger. Recommended: scale_factor = 0.05, threshold = 1000.

Adding error_count is fine (PG 11+ metadata-only with NOT NULL DEFAULT 0). But every error increment will UPDATE jobs, which combined with the partial index from line 4 is non-HOT — this column actively makes bloat worse. Consider storing error_count in a separate lighter-weight stats table or dropping the partial index.

Architectural pattern critique: PostgreSQL is a poor fit for a 6000 ops/sec job queue. Each dequeue is a SELECT...FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED + UPDATE = 2 row visibilities per claim. Consider Redis Streams, NATS JetStream, or a dedicated queue (SQS, RabbitMQ). If staying on PG, use partition-by-state pattern (separate physical tables per state) or LISTEN/NOTIFY for new-job pings.
